raw_arxiv_papers.abstract: In this paper we investigate the opportunities provided by the James Webb Space Telescope (JWST) for significant scientific advances in the study of solar system bodies and rings using stellar occultations. raw_arxiv_papers.title: Characterization of JWST NIRCam PSFs and Implications for AGN+Host Image Decomposition
raw_arxiv_papers.abstract: We present a detailed analysis of the point spread function (PSF) of JWST NIRCam imaging in eight filters: F070W, F115W, F150W, F200W, F277W, F356W, F444W, and F480M, using publicly available data. Spatial variations in the PSF FWHM generally decrease with wavelength: the maximum and RMS fractional variations are $\sim20\%$ and $5\%$ in F070W, reduced to $\sim3\%$ and $0.6\%$ in F444W. We compare three commonly-used methods (SWarp, photutils, and PSFEx) to construct model PSFs and conclude that PSFEx delivers the best performance. Using simulated images of broad-line AGNs, we evaluate the impact of PSF mismatches on the recoverability of host galaxy properties. Host fluxes are generally overestimated when adopting mismatched PSF models, with larger overestimation for more AGN-dominated sys

raw_arxiv_papers.title: Global sky background images for JWST/NIRISS Wide-Field Slitless Spectroscopy
raw_arxiv_papers.abstract: We present updated empirical background images for JWST/NIRISS Wide-Field Slitless Spectroscopy (WFSS) for both orthogonal grisms (GR150C, GR150R) crossed with all NIRISS WFSS wide-band filters (F090W, F115W, F150W, F200W). The background images are created using carefully vetted science and calibration exposures and improve the quality of previous background reference files. We present our methodology to create the background images and assess their quality using background subtracted science data. Overall, background residuals reach below 1% of the sky brightness for all filter and grism combinations, corresponding to a decrease in the background RMS of a factor of up to 7 from previous background reference files. The new background images are available on the Calibration Reference Data

raw_arxiv_papers.title: Faint galaxies in the Zone of Avoidance revealed by JWST/NIRCam
raw_arxiv_papers.abstract: The Zone of Avoidance (ZoA) remains one of the last frontiers in constructing a comprehensive three-dimensional map of the Universe. Galactic extinction, stellar crowding, and confusion noise have historically limited the detection of background galaxies in these regions, with implications for large-scale structure and cosmological measurements. We assess the capability of the James Webb Space Telescope (JWST) Near Infrared Camera (NIRCam) to detect extragalactic sources in a heavily contaminated region of the Milky Way. We analyzed JWST/NIRCam wide-filter images of NGC 3324 with a customized implementation of SExtractor v2.28. ## Agentic Orion Triage Report — Unknown

### Observation Summary
A blind survey conducted with JWST/NIRCam focused on high redshift candidates through imaging and spectroscopy. The observation, part of the CEERS GO #1345 program, identified a candidate with a photometric redshift of 11.8 and a spectroscopic redshift of 11.44 in the EGS field. The object has a magnitude of 27.3 in the F200W_AB band and a log stellar mass of 8.5. The observation is confirmed in arXiv: 2208.01612.

### Characterization
Key features include high redshift estimates and a significant stellar mass. Missing data includes spectroscopic confirmation of the redshift, additional photometric data in other bands, and temporal data to assess variability. Uncertainties exist in redshift classification and stellar mass estimation, with pipeline flags suggesting caution.

### Triage Verdict
**HIGH_PRIORITY** — Interest score: 0.85 / 1.0

### Competing Hypotheses
| Hypothesis | Confidence | Key Evidence For | Key Evidence Against |
|---|---|---|---|
| High-z Galaxy | 0.75 | Photometric and spectroscopic redshifts | Potential blending artefacts |
| Lensed Source | 0.15 | Astrophysical interpretation | Lack of lensing features |
| Merger or Starburst Galaxy | 0.10 | Astrophysical interpretation | No starburst or merger signatures |

### Branch Analysis
**Astrophysical branch**: The object is likely a high-z galaxy, supported by both photometric and spectroscopic redshifts. However, uncertainties in these measurements and stellar mass estimation pose classification risks.  
**Artefact branch**: There is a moderate probability (0.4) that the observation is an artefact, potentially due to photometric pipeline deblending failures.  
**Novelty/Rarity branch**: The candidate is extremely rare (rarity: 0.9) and novel (novelty: 0.8), with a high follow-up value (0.9) due to its potential to challenge existing galaxy formation models.  
**Context branch**: Literature confirms the observation's significance, but highlights the need for further verification to rule out artefacts.

### Scientific Disagreement
The Astrophysical Interpretation branch assigns high confidence to the high-z galaxy hypothesis, while the Artefact branch suggests a moderate probability of artefact due to deblending issues. This disagreement is critical as it affects the prioritization of follow-up observations.

### Timing
Parallel branches (wall): 12874 ms (vs. 27373 ms sequential → 2.13x speedup)

### Follow-Up Plan
1. Conduct follow-up spectroscopy to confirm redshift and check for blending artefacts using VLT/X-Shooter. This is immediate and crucial for confirming the high-z galaxy hypothesis.
2. Acquire additional multi-band imaging to search for lensing features or nearby sources using HST/ACS. This is urgent to support or refute the lensed source hypothesis.

### Quantitative Analysis
N/A

### References
[1] JWST observations of stellar occultations by solar system bodies and rings — http://arxiv.org/abs/1510.06575v3  
[2] Characterization of JWST NIRCam PSFs and Implications for AGN+Host Image Decomposition — http://arxiv.org/abs/2304.13776v1  
[3] Global sky background images for JWST/NIRISS Wide-Field Slitless Spectroscopy — http://arxiv.org/abs/2507.10650v1  
[4] Faint galaxies in the Zone of Avoidance revealed by JWST/NIRCam — http://arxiv.org/abs/2510.12488v1

raw_arxiv_papers.title: Characterization of JWST NIRCam PSFs and Implications for AGN+Host Image Decomposition
raw_arxiv_papers.abstract: We present a detailed analysis of the point spread function (PSF) of JWST NIRCam imaging in eight filters: F070W, F115W, F150W, F200W, F277W, F356W, F444W, and F480M, using publicly available data. Spatial variations in the PSF FWHM generally decrease with wavelength: the maximum and RMS fractional variations are $\sim20\%$ and $5\%$ in F070W, reduced to $\sim3\%$ and $0.6\%$ in F444W. We compare three commonly-used methods (SWarp, photutils, and PSFEx) to construct model PSFs and conclude that PSFEx delivers the best performance. Using simulated images of broad-line AGNs, we evaluate the impact of PSF mismatches on the recoverability of host galaxy properties. raw_arxiv_papers.abstract: We present updated empirical background images for JWST/NIRISS Wide-Field Slitless Spectroscopy (WFSS) for both orthogonal grisms (GR150C, GR150R) crossed with all NIRISS WFSS wide-band filters (F090W, F115W, F150W, F200W). The background images are created using carefully vetted science and calibration exposures and improve the quality of previous background reference files. We present our methodology to create the background images and assess their quality using background subtracted science data. Overall, background residuals reach below 1% of the sky brightness for all filter and grism combinations, corresponding to a decrease in the background RMS of a factor of up to 7 from previous background reference files.
